name: Atoms, nuclear decay, electronic structure, and atomic chemical behavior

nodes.label: Atomic Structure & Composition
nodes.summary: Atoms consist of a dense positive nucleus of protons and neutrons surrounded by electrons.

nodes.label: Subatomic Particles
nodes.summary: Protons, neutrons, and electrons are the three fundamental constituents of the atom.

nodes.label: Proton
nodes.summary: The proton carries a +1 elementary charge, has a mass of ~1 amu, and resides in the nucleus.

nodes.label: Neutron
nodes.summary: The neutron is electrically neutral, has a mass of ~1 amu, and resides in the nucleus.

nodes.label: Electron
nodes.summary: The electron carries a -1 elementary charge and has a mass ~1/1836 that of a proton.

nodes.label: Relative Particle Masses
nodes.summary: Nearly all atomic mass is in the nucleus because protons and neutrons are ~1836x heavier than electrons.

nodes.label: Elementary Charge
nodes.summary: The elementary charge e equals 1.6 x 10^-19 C, the magnitude of charge on a proton or electron.

nodes.label: Neutral Atom (Z protons = Z electrons)
nodes.summary: A neutral atom has equal numbers of protons and electrons, giving zero net charge.

nodes.label: The Nucleus
nodes.summary: The nucleus is the dense, positively charged core containing protons and neutrons (nucleons).

nodes.label: Nucleons
nodes.summary: Nucleons are the protons and neutrons bound together in the nucleus.

nodes.label: Nuclear Density & Size
nodes.summary: The nucleus is extremely small and dense relative to the atom, holding nearly all the mass in a tiny volume.

nodes.label: Strong Nuclear Force
nodes.summary: The strong nuclear force binds nucleons over very short ranges, overcoming proton-proton electrostatic repulsion.

nodes.label: Rutherford Gold-Foil Model
nodes.summary: Rutherford's gold-foil experiment showed atoms have a small, dense, positively charged nucleus with mostly empty space.

nodes.label: Atomic Notation & Definitions
nodes.summary: Atomic number, mass number, and isotopic notation define an atom's composition.

nodes.label: Atomic Number (Z)
nodes.summary: The atomic number Z equals the number of protons and uniquely defines the element.

nodes.label: Mass Number (A)
nodes.summary: The mass number A equals the total number of protons plus neutrons (A = Z + N).

nodes.label: Neutron Number (N = A − Z)
nodes.summary: The number of neutrons equals the mass number minus the atomic number.

nodes.label: Isotopic Notation
nodes.summary: An isotope is written as A superscript-Z subscript X, encoding mass number, atomic number, and element symbol.

nodes.label: Element Symbol
nodes.summary: Each element is denoted by a one- or two-letter symbol corresponding to a fixed atomic number.

nodes.label: Ions
nodes.summary: Ions are atoms with unequal numbers of protons and electrons, carrying net charge.

nodes.label: Cation
nodes.summary: A cation is a positively charged ion formed by losing one or more electrons.

nodes.label: Anion
nodes.summary: An anion is a negatively charged ion formed by gaining one or more electrons.

nodes.label: Determining Net Charge
nodes.summary: Net charge equals the number of protons minus the number of electrons.

nodes.label: Isotopes & Atomic Mass
nodes.summary: Isotopes are atoms of an element with differing neutron counts; atomic weight is their weighted average.

nodes.label: Definition of Isotopes
nodes.summary: Isotopes share atomic number but differ in mass number due to differing neutron numbers.

nodes.label: Isotopes Concept
nodes.summary: Isotopes of an element have the same number of protons but different numbers of neutrons.

nodes.label: Identical Chemical Behavior
nodes.summary: Isotopes of an element have nearly identical chemical behavior because they have the same electron configuration.

nodes.label: Hydrogen Isotopes
nodes.summary: Hydrogen's isotopes are protium (no neutrons), deuterium (one neutron), and tritium (two neutrons).

nodes.label: Atomic Mass Units
nodes.summary: Atomic mass is measured in unified atomic mass units defined relative to carbon-12.

nodes.label: Atomic Mass Unit (amu)
nodes.summary: One amu is defined as exactly 1/12 the mass of a carbon-12 atom.

nodes.label: Isotopic Mass
nodes.summary: The isotopic mass is the actual mass of a specific isotope, close to but not exactly equal to its mass number.

nodes.label: Atomic Weight
nodes.summary: Atomic weight is the abundance-weighted average mass of an element's naturally occurring isotopes.

nodes.label: Weighted-Average Calculation
nodes.summary: Atomic weight is computed as the sum of each isotope's mass times its fractional natural abundance.

nodes.label: Natural Abundance
nodes.summary: Natural abundance is the relative fraction in which each isotope occurs in nature.

nodes.label: Non-Integer Atomic Masses
nodes.summary: Periodic-table masses are non-integers because they average multiple isotopes by abundance.

nodes.label: Back-Calculating Abundance
nodes.summary: Given two isotope masses and the average atomic weight, the fractional abundances can be solved algebraically.

nodes.label: Mass Spectrometry of Isotopes
nodes.summary: A mass spectrum resolves isotopes by mass-to-charge ratio, with peak heights giving relative abundances.

nodes.label: Mole & Avogadro's Number
nodes.summary: The mole links atomic-scale mass to macroscopic gram quantities via Avogadro's number.

nodes.label: Avogadro's Number
nodes.summary: One mole contains 6.022 x 10^23 particles, Avogadro's number.

nodes.label: Molar Mass
nodes.summary: The molar mass in grams per mole is numerically equal to the atomic or molecular weight in amu.

nodes.label: Nuclear Decay & Reactions
nodes.summary: Unstable nuclei undergo radioactive decay or fission/fusion, transforming into other nuclides.

nodes.label: Nuclear Stability
nodes.summary: Nuclear stability depends on the neutron-to-proton ratio and total nucleon count.

nodes.label: Neutron-to-Proton Ratio
nodes.summary: Stable nuclei lie within a band of stability defined by an appropriate neutron-to-proton ratio.

nodes.label: Band of Stability
nodes.summary: Nuclei above the band undergo beta-minus decay and those below undergo positron emission or electron capture.

nodes.label: Heavy-Nucleus Instability
nodes.summary: Nuclei with Z greater than 83 are inherently unstable and tend to decay, often by alpha emission.

nodes.label: Nuclear Binding Energy
nodes.summary: Binding energy is the energy needed to disassemble a nucleus into its separate nucleons.

nodes.label: Mass Defect
nodes.summary: The mass defect is the difference between a nucleus's mass and the summed masses of its nucleons.

nodes.label: Mass-Energy Equivalence (E = mc^2)
nodes.summary: The mass defect is converted to binding energy via Einstein's relation E = mc^2.

nodes.label: Binding Energy per Nucleon
nodes.summary: Binding energy per nucleon peaks near iron-56, the most stable nuclide.

nodes.label: Radioactive Decay Modes
nodes.summary: Unstable nuclei emit alpha, beta, gamma, or positron radiation to gain stability.

nodes.label: Alpha Decay
nodes.summary: Alpha decay emits a helium-4 nucleus, decreasing the mass number by 4 and atomic number by 2.

nodes.label: Alpha Particle Identity
nodes.summary: An alpha particle is a helium-4 nucleus, two protons and two neutrons with a +2 charge.

nodes.label: Beta-Minus Decay
nodes.summary: Beta-minus decay converts a neutron to a proton, emitting an electron and increasing Z by 1.

nodes.label: Beta Particle Identity
nodes.summary: A beta-minus particle is a high-speed electron emitted from the nucleus, with mass number 0 and charge -1.

nodes.label: Beta-Plus (Positron) Decay
nodes.summary: Positron emission converts a proton to a neutron, emitting a positron and decreasing Z by 1.

nodes.label: Gamma Decay
nodes.summary: Gamma decay releases a high-energy photon from an excited nucleus without changing A or Z.

nodes.label: Electron Capture
nodes.summary: Electron capture absorbs an inner electron, converting a proton to a neutron and decreasing Z by 1.

nodes.label: The Positron
nodes.summary: A positron is the antimatter counterpart of an electron with +1 charge and equal mass.

nodes.label: Decay Series & Daughter Chains
nodes.summary: An unstable nuclide may decay through a sequence of steps until reaching a stable daughter isotope.

nodes.label: Penetrating Power of Radiation
nodes.summary: Alpha is least penetrating, beta intermediate, and gamma most penetrating.

nodes.label: Ionizing Radiation & Biological Effect
nodes.summary: Ionizing radiation ejects electrons from atoms; alpha is most ionizing per distance, gamma least but most penetrating.

nodes.label: Balancing Nuclear Equations
nodes.summary: Nuclear equations balance conserved mass number and atomic number on both sides.

nodes.label: Conservation of A and Z
nodes.summary: In a nuclear reaction the total mass number and total charge (atomic number) are conserved.

nodes.label: Identifying Decay Products
nodes.summary: The daughter nuclide is found by conserving A and Z across the decay equation.

nodes.label: Nuclear Transmutation
nodes.summary: Transmutation changes one element into another, either by natural decay or by particle bombardment.

nodes.label: Decay Kinetics & Half-Life
nodes.summary: Radioactive decay is a first-order process characterized by a constant half-life.

nodes.label: First-Order Decay
nodes.summary: Radioactive decay follows first-order kinetics, with rate proportional to the number of nuclei present.

nodes.label: Half-Life
nodes.summary: The half-life is the time for half of a radioactive sample to decay.

nodes.label: Exponential Decay Equation
nodes.summary: Remaining amount equals the initial amount times (1/2) raised to the number of elapsed half-lives.

nodes.label: Decay Constant
nodes.summary: The decay constant relates to half-life by t-half = ln(2)/lambda.

nodes.label: Activity & Decay Rate
nodes.summary: Activity is the number of decays per unit time, equal to lambda times the number of nuclei present.

nodes.label: Radiometric Dating
nodes.summary: Comparing remaining isotope fraction to half-life dates samples, as in carbon-14 dating.

nodes.label: Fission & Fusion
nodes.summary: Fission splits heavy nuclei and fusion combines light nuclei, both releasing nuclear energy.

nodes.label: Nuclear Fission
nodes.summary: Fission splits a heavy nucleus into lighter nuclei, releasing energy and neutrons.

nodes.label: Nuclear Fusion
nodes.summary: Fusion combines light nuclei into a heavier nucleus, releasing large amounts of energy.

nodes.label: Energy from Mass Loss
nodes.summary: Both fission and fusion release energy because the products have a higher binding energy per nucleon.

nodes.label: Bohr Model & Atomic Spectra
nodes.summary: The Bohr model quantizes electron orbits and explains discrete atomic emission and absorption spectra.

nodes.label: Quantized Energy Levels
nodes.summary: Electrons occupy discrete, quantized energy levels around the nucleus.

nodes.label: Bohr's Postulate
nodes.summary: Bohr proposed electrons travel in fixed circular orbits of quantized angular momentum and energy.

nodes.label: Quantized Angular Momentum
nodes.summary: Bohr required orbital angular momentum to be an integer multiple of h/2pi, fixing the allowed orbits.

nodes.label: Hydrogen Energy Levels
nodes.summary: Hydrogen energy levels follow E = -13.6 eV / n^2, becoming less negative at higher n.

nodes.label: Ionization Energy of Hydrogen
nodes.summary: Removing hydrogen's electron from the ground state (n=1 to infinity) requires 13.6 eV.

nodes.label: Ground vs. Excited States
nodes.summary: The ground state is the lowest-energy configuration; excited states have electrons in higher levels.

nodes.label: Electronic Transitions
nodes.summary: Electrons absorb or emit photons whose energy equals the gap between two levels.

nodes.label: Photon Absorption
nodes.summary: An electron jumps to a higher level by absorbing a photon of exactly the energy-gap value.

nodes.label: Photon Emission
nodes.summary: An electron falling to a lower level emits a photon equal to the energy difference.

nodes.label: Transition Energy (ΔE = hf)
nodes.summary: The photon energy of a transition equals the difference between the two level energies, E = hf.

nodes.label: Planck's Relation (E = hf)
nodes.summary: Photon energy is proportional to frequency through Planck's constant, E = hf = hc/lambda.

nodes.label: The Photon (Quantized Light)
nodes.summary: Light is quantized into photons, discrete packets of energy that carry electromagnetic radiation.

nodes.label: Atomic Spectra
nodes.summary: Discrete spectral lines arise from quantized electron transitions, fingerprinting each element.

nodes.label: Emission Spectrum
nodes.summary: An emission spectrum shows bright lines from photons released as electrons drop to lower levels.

nodes.label: Absorption Spectrum
nodes.summary: An absorption spectrum shows dark lines where electrons absorb specific photon energies.

nodes.label: Discrete Line Spectra
nodes.summary: Atomic spectra are discrete lines, not continuous, because energy levels are quantized.

nodes.label: Spectra as Element Fingerprints
nodes.summary: Each element emits a unique set of spectral lines, allowing spectroscopic identification.

nodes.label: Rydberg Formula
nodes.summary: The Rydberg formula gives hydrogen spectral-line wavelengths from the two principal quantum numbers.

nodes.label: Hydrogen Spectral Series
nodes.summary: Lyman (UV), Balmer (visible), and Paschen (IR) series correspond to transitions ending at n=1, 2, and 3.

nodes.label: Photon-Matter Interactions
nodes.summary: The photoelectric effect and related phenomena reveal light's quantized, particle-like behavior.

nodes.label: Photoelectric Effect
nodes.summary: Light above a threshold frequency ejects electrons from a metal, demonstrating the particle nature of light.

nodes.label: Work Function & Threshold Frequency
nodes.summary: The work function is the minimum photon energy needed to eject an electron; excess energy becomes kinetic energy.

nodes.label: Fluorescence & Phosphorescence
nodes.summary: Absorbed light can be re-emitted at longer wavelength as fluorescence (fast) or phosphorescence (delayed).

nodes.label: Quantum Mechanical Model
nodes.summary: The quantum model describes electrons by probability orbitals defined by four quantum numbers.

nodes.label: Wave-Particle Duality
nodes.summary: Electrons exhibit both particle and wave behavior, described probabilistically.

nodes.label: de Broglie Wavelength
nodes.summary: Matter has a wavelength lambda = h/(mv), significant for small fast particles like electrons.

nodes.label: Heisenberg Uncertainty Principle
nodes.summary: One cannot simultaneously know an electron's exact position and momentum with arbitrary precision.

nodes.label: Orbital as Probability Cloud
nodes.summary: An orbital is a region of space where an electron is most probably found, not a fixed orbit.

nodes.label: The Four Quantum Numbers
nodes.summary: Each electron is uniquely described by n, l, m-l, and m-s.

nodes.label: Principal Quantum Number (n)
nodes.summary: The principal quantum number n gives the shell and primary energy and size; n = 1, 2, 3, ...

nodes.label: Azimuthal Quantum Number (l)
nodes.summary: The azimuthal number l (0 to n-1) defines the subshell and orbital shape (s, p, d, f).

nodes.label: Magnetic Quantum Number (m_l)
nodes.summary: The magnetic number m-l ranges from -l to +l and specifies an orbital's spatial orientation.

nodes.label: Spin Quantum Number (m_s)
nodes.summary: The spin number m-s is +1/2 or -1/2, describing an electron's intrinsic spin orientation.

nodes.label: Allowed Quantum-Number Sets
nodes.summary: Valid sets require l < n and -l <= m-l <= l, so impossible combinations can be ruled out.

nodes.label: Orbitals & Electrons per Subshell
nodes.summary: Each subshell holds 2l+1 orbitals, and each orbital holds 2 electrons (max 2, 6, 10, 14 for s,p,d,f).

nodes.label: Shell Electron Capacity (2n^2)
nodes.summary: A shell of principal number n holds a maximum of 2n^2 electrons.

nodes.label: Orbital Shapes
nodes.summary: s, p, d, and f orbitals have characteristic spatial shapes.

nodes.label: s Orbital
nodes.summary: The s orbital is spherical and holds up to 2 electrons.

nodes.label: p Orbital
nodes.summary: p orbitals are dumbbell-shaped, three per subshell, holding up to 6 electrons.

nodes.label: d and f Orbitals
nodes.summary: d orbitals hold up to 10 and f orbitals up to 14 electrons with more complex shapes.

nodes.label: Degenerate Orbitals
nodes.summary: Orbitals within the same subshell are degenerate, having equal energy in an isolated atom.

nodes.label: Nodes in Orbitals
nodes.summary: A node is a region of zero electron probability; the number of nodes increases with n and l.

nodes.label: Electron Configuration
nodes.summary: Electron configuration assigns electrons to orbitals by energy following established filling rules.

nodes.label: Filling Rules
nodes.summary: The Aufbau principle, Pauli exclusion, and Hund's rule govern orbital filling order.

nodes.label: Aufbau Principle
nodes.summary: Electrons fill the lowest-energy orbitals first before occupying higher ones.

nodes.label: Pauli Exclusion Principle
nodes.summary: No two electrons in an atom can share all four quantum numbers, so an orbital holds at most two opposite-spin electrons.

nodes.label: Hund's Rule
nodes.summary: Electrons singly occupy degenerate orbitals with parallel spins before pairing.

nodes.label: (n + l) Energy Ordering
nodes.summary: Orbital energy increases with n + l, and ties are broken by lower n filling first.

nodes.label: Diagonal (Madelung) Filling Order
nodes.summary: The diagonal rule predicts filling order 1s 2s 2p 3s 3p 4s 3d 4p and so on.

nodes.label: Configuration Notation
nodes.summary: Configurations are written in spdf or noble-gas-core shorthand, with orbital diagrams showing spins.

nodes.label: spdf Notation
nodes.summary: Configurations are written as subshell symbols with superscripts for electron count, e.g. 1s2 2s2 2p6.

nodes.label: Noble-Gas Shorthand
nodes.summary: Configurations abbreviate inner electrons with the preceding noble gas in brackets.

nodes.label: Orbital (Box) Diagrams
nodes.summary: Orbital diagrams use boxes and arrows to depict electron occupancy and spin.

nodes.label: Valence vs. Core Electrons
nodes.summary: Valence electrons in the outermost shell govern chemistry; core electrons are inner and inert.

nodes.label: Valence Electrons
nodes.summary: Valence electrons occupy the highest principal energy level and determine bonding behavior.

nodes.label: Core Electrons
nodes.summary: Core electrons are inner-shell electrons that do not participate in bonding.

nodes.label: Group Number Predicts Valence
nodes.summary: For main-group elements, the group number equals the number of valence electrons.

nodes.label: Octet Rule & Stable Configurations
nodes.summary: Atoms gain, lose, or share electrons to reach a stable full-octet (noble-gas) valence configuration.

nodes.label: Predicting Common Ion Charges
nodes.summary: Main-group ion charges follow from reaching the nearest noble-gas configuration (e.g. Na+, Mg2+, O2-).

nodes.label: Configuration Anomalies
nodes.summary: Chromium and copper deviate from Aufbau order for added stability.

nodes.label: Chromium & Copper Exceptions
nodes.summary: Cr and Cu promote an s electron to d for the stability of half-filled and fully filled d subshells.

nodes.label: Half-Filled & Full-Subshell Stability
nodes.summary: Half-filled and completely filled subshells have extra stability driving configuration anomalies.

nodes.label: Configurations of Ions
nodes.summary: Ions form by removing or adding electrons, with cations losing highest-n electrons first.

nodes.label: Cation Configurations
nodes.summary: Cations lose electrons from the highest principal level first, so transition metals lose s before d.

nodes.label: Anion Configurations
nodes.summary: Anions gain electrons into the next available orbital, often reaching a noble-gas configuration.

nodes.label: Isoelectronic Species
nodes.summary: Isoelectronic species have identical electron configurations despite differing nuclear charges.

nodes.label: Paramagnetism & Diamagnetism
nodes.summary: Unpaired electrons make a species paramagnetic; all-paired electrons make it diamagnetic.

nodes.label: Paramagnetism
nodes.summary: A paramagnetic species has unpaired electrons and is attracted to a magnetic field.

nodes.label: Diamagnetism
nodes.summary: A diamagnetic species has all paired electrons and is weakly repelled by a magnetic field.

nodes.label: Periodic Table & Periodic Trends
nodes.summary: The periodic table organizes elements by atomic number, and periodic trends follow electron structure.

nodes.label: Organization of the Table
nodes.summary: Elements are arranged in periods and groups reflecting recurring electron configurations.

nodes.label: Periodic Law
nodes.summary: Element properties are periodic functions of atomic number.

nodes.label: Periods and Groups
nodes.summary: Rows are periods sharing the highest n; columns are groups sharing valence configuration.

nodes.label: s, p, d, f Blocks
nodes.summary: The table divides into s, p, d, and f blocks based on the subshell being filled.

nodes.label: Representative vs. Transition Elements
nodes.summary: Representative (main-group) elements fill s and p subshells; transition elements fill d subshells.

nodes.label: Element Classifications
nodes.summary: Elements are grouped as metals, nonmetals, metalloids, and named families.

nodes.label: Metals
nodes.summary: Metals are typically lustrous, conductive, malleable, and tend to lose electrons forming cations.

nodes.label: Nonmetals
nodes.summary: Nonmetals are poor conductors that tend to gain electrons forming anions.

nodes.label: Metalloids
nodes.summary: Metalloids border the metal/nonmetal staircase and have intermediate properties (semiconductors).

nodes.label: Alkali Metals
nodes.summary: Group 1 alkali metals have one valence electron and are highly reactive, forming +1 cations.

nodes.label: Alkaline Earth Metals
nodes.summary: Group 2 alkaline earth metals have two valence electrons and form +2 cations.

nodes.label: Transition Metals
nodes.summary: Transition metals fill d subshells and commonly show variable oxidation states and colored complexes.

nodes.label: Lanthanides & Actinides
nodes.summary: The inner-transition lanthanides and actinides fill f subshells and sit below the main table.

nodes.label: Halogens
nodes.summary: Group 17 halogens have seven valence electrons and are highly reactive, forming -1 anions.

nodes.label: Noble Gases
nodes.summary: Group 18 noble gases have full valence shells and are largely chemically inert.

nodes.label: Chalcogens & Pnictogens
nodes.summary: Group 16 chalcogens (6 valence e-) and group 15 pnictogens (5 valence e-) are reactive nonmetal families.

nodes.label: Effective Nuclear Charge & Shielding
nodes.summary: Effective nuclear charge is the net positive pull on valence electrons after core shielding.

nodes.label: Effective Nuclear Charge (Z_eff)
nodes.summary: Z-eff is the net nuclear charge felt by a valence electron, approximately Z minus core electrons.

nodes.label: Electron Shielding
nodes.summary: Inner electrons shield outer electrons, reducing the effective nuclear charge they experience.

nodes.label: Z_eff Periodic Trend
nodes.summary: Z-eff increases across a period and changes little down a group, driving most periodic trends.

nodes.label: Atomic & Ionic Radius
nodes.summary: Atomic size decreases across a period and increases down a group; ions differ predictably from atoms.

nodes.label: Atomic Radius Trend
nodes.summary: Atomic radius decreases left-to-right across a period and increases top-to-bottom down a group.

nodes.label: Cation Radius
nodes.summary: Cations are smaller than their parent atoms due to fewer electrons and stronger per-electron pull.

nodes.label: Anion Radius
nodes.summary: Anions are larger than their parent atoms due to added electron-electron repulsion.

nodes.label: Isoelectronic Radius Trend
nodes.summary: Among isoelectronic species, greater nuclear charge gives a smaller radius.

nodes.label: Ionization Energy
nodes.summary: Ionization energy is the energy to remove an electron and rises with effective nuclear charge.

nodes.label: Ionization Energy Definition
nodes.summary: Ionization energy is the energy required to remove the most loosely held electron from a gaseous atom.

nodes.label: Ionization Energy Trend
nodes.summary: Ionization energy increases across a period and decreases down a group.

nodes.label: Successive Ionization Energies
nodes.summary: Each successive ionization energy is larger, with a large jump after removing all valence electrons.

nodes.label: IE Trend Irregularities
nodes.summary: Small dips occur after group 2 (s-to-p) and group 15 (pairing), exceptions to the smooth trend.

nodes.label: Electron Affinity
nodes.summary: Electron affinity is the energy change when an atom gains an electron, generally exothermic.

nodes.label: Electron Affinity Definition
nodes.summary: Electron affinity is the energy released when a gaseous atom gains an electron.

nodes.label: Electron Affinity Trend
nodes.summary: Electron affinity generally becomes more exothermic across a period (excluding noble gases).

nodes.label: Electronegativity
nodes.summary: Electronegativity measures an atom's pull on shared bonding electrons, peaking at fluorine.

nodes.label: Electronegativity Definition
nodes.summary: Electronegativity is the tendency of a bonded atom to attract shared electrons.

nodes.label: Electronegativity Trend
nodes.summary: Electronegativity increases across a period and decreases down a group, highest for fluorine.

nodes.label: Pauling Scale
nodes.summary: The Pauling scale quantifies electronegativity, assigning fluorine the highest value (~4.0).

nodes.label: Metallic Character & Reactivity
nodes.summary: Metallic character and reactivity trends follow ionization energy and electronegativity patterns.

nodes.label: Metallic Character Trend
nodes.summary: Metallic character increases down a group and decreases across a period.

nodes.label: Reactivity Trends
nodes.summary: Metal reactivity rises down a group; nonmetal reactivity rises up a group toward fluorine.

nodes.label: Acidic vs. Basic Oxides
nodes.summary: Metal oxides tend to be basic and nonmetal oxides acidic, tracking metallic character.

nodes.label: Oxidation States from Periodicity
nodes.summary: Common oxidation states follow group position and valence electron count.

nodes.label: Group-Predicted Oxidation States
nodes.summary: Main-group elements show characteristic oxidation states tied to gaining or losing valence electrons to an octet.

nodes.label: Variable Oxidation States
nodes.summary: Transition metals adopt multiple oxidation states because s and d electrons have similar energies.

external_prereqs.concept_hint: energy, work, and conservation of energy
first-order reaction rate law and rate constant
electromagnetic waves: frequency, wavelength, and the speed of light
properties of waves and the photon/wave nature of light
the photon nature of light and electromagnetic spectrum
Coulomb's law: electrostatic attraction and distance
stoichiometry and the mole concept in reactions
